metadata.title: Briu | Brand in a Session
metadata.text:
Briu | Brand in a Session 

 Build

 Brand in a Session

 400+ Midjourney generations. Hundreds of name candidates. One logo. How agents and founder taste produced a brand identity in hours, not months. The problem

 Branding agency. 6-8 week discovery. $15,000-25,000. For a company proving agents can replace expensive outsourced work, that felt like the wrong way to start. Finding the name

 The search started in Claude. Geographic roots, Gaelic and Celtic patterns, abstract concepts, short available domains. The agent generated hundreds of candidates, each checked against domain availability, trademark risk, pronunciation, and brand fit. We built an OpenClaw skill mid-session to automate domain search: whois lookups, .com and .ai availability, trademark screening through USPTO. 50+ candidates got the full treatment. "Briu" came from Catalan. The word works two ways: as a noun meaning energy, push, courage (synonyms include energia , empenta , coratge , ànim ) and as an interjection used to urge action, like "let's go."

 Briu isn't a word you'd hear in a Barcelona coffee shop. It's older than that. In modern street Catalan, people say empenta for drive or fot-li canya for step on it. But briu persists in the literary tradition, in the northern Rossellonès dialect where it's still spoken naturally, and in its cousin languages: "brio" in Italian and English, "brío" in Spanish. It traces back to Proto-Celtic roots meaning strength. We chose it because it carries weight without shouting. Energy, push, courage.

metadata.title: Briu | Brand in a Session
metadata.text:
A Catalan word that led us to Gaudi, Barcelona, and the visual language that became our brand. The .ai was available. No trademark conflicts. Short, pronounceable, didn't sound like every other AI company. The name did something else we didn't plan for. A Catalan word naturally pointed toward Barcelona, and Barcelona pointed toward Gaudi. The name and the design language share the same root. Cost: a few dollars in API across 50+ candidates. $85 for the domain. The visual identity

 The same Claude thread that generated names was also generating Midjourney prompts. It had the context (the Catalan origin, the Gaudi connection, the brand personality) so we used it to write the prompts, and then screenshotted the results back into the thread for selection. The loop was: Claude writes prompts, Midjourney generates a batch, founder screenshots the results back, Claude and founder both pick favorites (often the same ones), and that feeds the next round of prompts. The first runs were wide open. B lettermarks, flames, arrows, chevrons, abstract symbols, stars. Everything on the table. Claude was prompting across dozens of directions at once to map the space. Then the arch appeared. Clean gold line-art arches on black. Concentric, nested, pointed. Mountains and trees kept getting tested alongside, but the arch kept winning every round. We pushed hard on variations: line weight, symmetry, layering, pointed vs. rounded, with and without a base. Once the arch locked in, the Gaudi influence came back in. We layered mosaic texture onto the arch form.

metadata.title: Briu | Brand in a Session
metadata.text:
Teal, copper, gold tilework inspired by Sagrada Familia and Park Guell. This phase had the longest iteration run. Dozens of near-identical generations with subtle differences in color balance, arch proportion, and texture density. Final mark: a Gaudi-inspired mosaic arch, assembled in Canva. Proportions, dark background testing, 16px favicon to full-page hero. 400+ generations. One evening. Watch the brand evolve

 Scroll right to follow the evolution 

 Phase 1: Wide Open

 B lettermarks, flames, arrows, chevrons, stars, lightning bolts. Mapping every possible direction at once. Phase 2: Narrowing

 Ornate symbols, spade and arch shapes, mountains, Celtic knots. The arch and pointed forms start surfacing. Phase 3: Two Threads

 Clean line-art arches emerge alongside tree of life explorations and heavy mosaic B letterforms. Two directions competing. Phase 4: The Arch Wins

 Clean gold arches on black lock in. Mountains, trees, and flames still tested, but the arch beats everything. Variations on line weight, symmetry, layering. Phase 5: Mosaic Layered In

 Gaudi texture returns. Teal and copper mosaic tilework layered onto the arch form. Rich color, cathedral window shapes. The two threads merge. Phase 6: Deep Refinement

 Longest iteration run. Dozens of near-identical mosaic arches. Subtle differences in color balance, proportion, texture density. Homing in. Phase 7: Final Convergence

 The mark settles. Pointed mosaic arches, teal and copper on dark. Consistent form, consistent palette. Ready for Canva. All 400+ generations were created on Midjourney. Where we ended up.

metadata.title: Briu | Brand in a Session
metadata.text:
A layered arch in warm gold tones. The Gaudi mosaic refined into a clean mark. Assembled in Canva, tested at 16px favicon size and full-page hero size, exported as a transparent PNG. What this cost

 Traditional: $15,000-25,000 for name, visual identity, brand guidelines. 6-8 weeks. Item Cost 

 Midjourney subscription ~$30/month 

 Domain registration (briu.ai) $85 

 Claude (name generation, strategy) Included in $200/mo Max plan 

 OpenClaw (domain search across 50+ candidates) ~$5 in API 

 Canva Free tier 

 Total (direct costs) ~$120 

 Timeline: one evening for the visual identity. One session for the name and domain. What surprised us

 Claude could actually pick winners. When we screenshotted a batch of generations back into the thread, it identified the strongest candidates, and generally picked the same ones the founder would have. It could articulate why: "that one works as a brand mark because the form is distinctive at small sizes" or "the arch reads better than the tree because it has more inherent symmetry." In the early phases, most generations are junk, so the selection is obvious. But even as the iterations got tighter, Claude was picking for the right reasons. What the agent couldn't do was set the direction. It couldn't decide that the arch was more meaningful than the mountain for this brand. It couldn't feel when the mosaic texture went from "interesting" to "right." The founder made every strategic call. Which threads to pursue, when to abandon a direction, when to push deeper.

metadata.title: Briu | Brand in a Session
metadata.text:
The agent wrote the prompts, evaluated the results, and kept the iteration loop moving fast. That loop ran hundreds of times in one evening. The takeaway

 One creative arc. The agent searched hundreds of names across multiple languages and landed on a Catalan word. That word pointed to a visual world. Gaudi, Barcelona, arches, mosaics. The founder directed Midjourney through 400+ iterations refining that visual world into a clean mark, then finalized it in Canva. Name to logo, orchestrated by a human using agents at every step. The $120 brand is live at briu.ai right now. Back to Build

metadata.title: Briu | From Comment to Fix in One Loop
metadata.text:
Briu | From Comment to Fix in One Loop 

 Build

 From Comment to Fix in One Loop

 How we wired a customer feedback notification into an agent that reads the conversation, updates the live product, and emails the client, without us re-learning the account. The Real Cost of a Customer Comment

 We run chatbot deployments for clients through Subsights. A customer leaves a comment on one of their conversations: the opener is too narrow, a product line isn't triggering, the staff contact listed is someone who left last month. The fix itself is five minutes. But the fix isn't what takes the time. The context reload is what kills you. Log into the Subsights dashboard. Find the right workspace. Which client account was this again? Find the right chatbot. There might be three per workspace. Navigate through accordion menus to the prompt editor. Re-read the configuration to remember what you set up two months ago. Parse the customer's comment to understand what they're actually asking for. Make the change in the right textarea. Test it. Then figure out who to email: the ops lead or the founder? Is that email address even current? Write the email. Send it. Mark the comment as handled so nobody else picks it up. That's not five minutes. That's twenty to thirty. And for a small team, that friction means comments sit for days. Not because the work is hard. Because starting the work is hard.

metadata.title: Briu | From Comment to Fix in One Loop
metadata.text:
Manual

 20-30 min

 Context reload, platform navigation, change, test, find contact, write email, mark handled

 Automated Loop

 ~2 min

 Notification fires, agent runs the full cycle, human reviews the email before send

 The Loop We Built

 Subsights posts a webhook to a Discord channel whenever a customer comments on a conversation. A monitoring script checks that channel every 30 minutes. Here's exactly what happens when it finds one:

 Detect & Dedup

 Script pulls new comments from the Discord webhook channel. Cross-references a SQLite table of already-processed comments. New ones get queued. Read Conversation

 Agent opens the actual customer conversation via browser automation. Reads the full thread: what the customer said, what the chatbot said, where it went wrong. Read Live Config

 Navigates the Subsights dashboard: workspace switching, accordion menus, prompt editor. Reads the current chatbot configuration so it knows exactly what to change. Make Changes

 Edits the prompts in the platform's textareas. Broader opener, new product triggers, updated staff contact, whatever the feedback requires. Verify

 Saves, reloads the configuration page, confirms the changes persisted. No "I think I saved it." The agent checks. Client Email

 Queries the client_contacts database for the right person at that workspace. Drafts a specific email explaining what changed and why. Sends on human approval. Close the Loop

 Marks the comment as processed in SQLite. Posts a status update back to the Discord thread. The next comment starts a fresh cycle.

metadata.title: Briu | From Comment to Fix in One Loop
metadata.text:
The whole cycle runs from a single heartbeat cron. The agent already knows the workspace structure, the contact database, the navigation pattern for each platform section. It doesn't need to re-learn the account because the account context lives in queryable database tables, not in someone's head. What Broke and What We Documented

 It didn't work cleanly the first time. Or the second. Every failure became a permanent upgrade to the system. Wrong chatbot name

 Workspace had three chatbots with similar names. Agent picked the wrong one and edited the wrong prompt. Fix: Built a client_chatbots database table mapping workspace IDs to the correct chatbot. Replaced hardcoded chatbot references across 6 files with one queryable source. Accordion menus and nested UI

 The Subsights prompt editor has collapsible sections. Agent couldn't find the right textarea because it was hidden inside a collapsed panel. Fix: Browser navigation guide updated with explicit expand-then-edit sequences for each section type. Different DOM selectors per workspace version. Stale contact information

 Commenter's email belonged to a former employee who had left the company. Agent would have emailed someone who no longer works there. Fix: Agent now cross-references the client_contacts table and validates against Gmail history. Found the real current contact before sending. Each failure became a permanent system upgrade. The client_chatbots table, the client_contacts database, the browser navigation guide, the comment processing script. They all get more reliable with every cycle.

metadata.title: Briu | From Comment to Fix in One Loop
metadata.text:
The system learns from its mistakes once. A human re-learns them every time they context-switch back into an account. What It Feels Like Now

 The heartbeat cron runs every 30 minutes. Comments come in. The loop runs. Changes go live. The client gets an email explaining exactly what was updated and why. A status update posts back to the Discord thread. The account context that used to live in someone's head, like which chatbot, which workspace, who's the contact, what did we configure last time, now lives in shared database tables ( client_chatbots , client_contacts ) and a browser navigation guide. The agent queries them every time. It doesn't have good days and bad days. It doesn't forget that the contact changed last month. It doesn't dread the small task that takes longer to start than to finish. The human role shifted from "do the whole thing" to "review the email before it sends." That's the right division. The agent handles the mechanical recall and execution. The human handles the judgment call on tone and correctness. The Knowledge Coherence Problem

 Before the loop, operational context was scattered. Chatbot IDs were hardcoded across 6 different files. Client contacts lived in email threads. Navigation patterns lived in muscle memory. Every time the agent (or a human) needed to service an account, they had to reconstruct this context from scratch. The loop forced us to consolidate. We built shared database tables. client_chatbots maps every workspace to its correct chatbot ID.

metadata.title: Briu | From Comment to Fix in One Loop
metadata.text:
client_contacts stores the current point of contact for each account, validated against real email history. The browser navigation guide documents the exact click sequence for each workspace type. This is the real output of the project. Not just the automation, but the structured knowledge that makes the automation possible. The loop runs because the context is encoded. And once it's encoded, it's available to every other process that touches that account. The comment loop was the forcing function. The knowledge system is the lasting asset. Why This Matters Beyond Us

 Every service business has this pattern. Small customer requests that are easy to do but painful to start. The fix is never the bottleneck. The context reload is. Think about the tasks your team dreads, not because they're hard, but because they require re-acquainting with an account, a platform, a relationship. Those are the tasks that sit in a queue for days. Those are the tasks that make customers feel forgotten. The pattern is straightforward:

 Encode the context. Contacts, credentials, navigation patterns, preferences, configuration history. Get it out of people's heads and into structured reference documents. Wire the trigger. Notification, email, form submission, webhook, whatever event signals "a customer needs something."

 Connect trigger to resolution. The agent reads the trigger, consults the context, executes the fix, communicates the result. The loop eliminates the context switch. The human stays in the loop for judgment. The customer gets a response in minutes instead of days.

metadata.title: Briu | From Comment to Fix in One Loop
metadata.text:
That's the service we deploy for clients. Not the agent itself. The elimination of the context reload that makes small tasks feel big. Back to Build

metadata.title: Briu | Starting a Company with Agents
metadata.text:
Briu | Starting a Company with Agents 

 Build

 Starting a Company with Agents

 From first agent skill to company launch. Exact toolchain, costs, and what the agent can't do. Specific enough that you could replicate it. Why We Started Building

 Jason Calacanis on the All-In Podcast: "Every week 5 to 10% of the work we're doing inside of our venture firm is being moved over to OpenClaw." That number is cumulative and permanent. Move 5% per week, and in 20 weeks you have automated everything you currently do by hand. The agent

 Before Briu existed, there was an agent built through Subsights, our previous business. It started simple: processing inbound email, triaging messages, drafting responses for review. Then it expanded. Calendar management. Daily reporting. CRM updates through HubSpot. Prospecting through Apollo. Research through web access. Each new skill followed the same process: build, test in isolation, connect to live systems with approval gates, monitor output, gradually reduce oversight as trust was earned. The agent never takes an external action without human approval. It can draft an email. It can't send one. It can propose a calendar change. It can't confirm one. Today, I don't go into my email. I don't manage my calendar. Those things are done for me, reviewed by me, and executed on my approval. The toolchain

 Claude Max Plan

 $200/month . Primary reasoning interface for strategic work: positioning, copy, analysis, planning. Claude Code

 Agent-powered development in the terminal. The entire Briu website was built this way.

metadata.title: Briu | Starting a Company with Agents
metadata.text:
HTML, CSS, JavaScript, responsive layouts, interactive components. OpenClaw Agent

 The orchestration layer. Manages the agent's skills, system connections, daily routines, and task execution. Email, calendar, CRM, web access, reporting. All structured as skills with defined inputs, outputs, and approval gates. Security

 When your agent is connected to your email, CRM, calendar, and customer data, the security model is the foundation. Every skill is built in-house. Every system connection has defined scopes and permissions. We built an internal security agent whose sole job is to audit the primary agent's access patterns and flag anomalies. The architecture is multi-agent: the primary agent handles high-trust tasks on the most capable model, isolated sub-agents handle lower-risk work on cost-effective options. Frontier-tier models for anything touching external data or customer information. Cheaper models for internal summarization, formatting, reporting. Costs and timeline

 First skill to production-ready system: three weeks. $376 in API costs

 OpenClaw runtime across all 12 skills: email, calendar, security, web access, HubSpot, Apollo, customer reports, demo creation. Claude Max: $200/mo 

 Shared platform across everything: agent strategy, Claude Code for the website, brand direction, planning. Not attributable to one workstream. ROI

 The agent handles work that would require multiple part-time hires. Ongoing API cost is a fraction of a single contractor. "When do tokens outpace the salary of the employee?

metadata.title: Briu | Starting a Company with Agents
metadata.text:
Because you're about to hit it." For most businesses, we are nowhere near that threshold. Building the brand with the stack

 Once the agent system was stable, we used the same stack to build the company. Briu was built with the tooling from day one. Name and domain. Claude explored naming directions across multiple languages. Hundreds of candidates, each evaluated against availability, trademark risk, and brand fit. We built an OpenClaw skill mid-process to automate domain search across 50+ candidates. "Briu" emerged from Catalan, a word meaning energy, push, courage. A human made the final call. Visual identity. 400 + Midjourney generations. Each round directed by founder taste, informed by the previous generation. The Catalan origin pointed toward Gaudi, and that aesthetic (pointed arches, mosaic tilework, warm gold) became the visual starting point. The logo was assembled in Canva and the brand system carried directly into the website build. Website. Built through Claude Code in the terminal. Describe what a section should accomplish, the agent writes the code, review in the browser, adjust in real time. The contact modal, service tiers, responsive navigation, economics charts. All built this way. Content. Site copy went through dozens of iterations. Claude on the web for strategic thinking. Claude Code for implementation. Every quote sourced with episode-level attribution. What the agent can't do

 The agent produced technically excellent work that completely missed the point. A services page that positioned Briu like an enterprise vendor. A homepage that felt cold.

metadata.title: Briu | Starting a Company with Agents
metadata.text:
A pricing section that sent the wrong signal about who we serve. The agent can't feel the gap between "correct" and "right." The goal isn't full automation. The goal is a system where the human spends time on judgment instead of production. The pattern we deploy for clients

 1. Identify the workflow

 Find the repeatable process that consumes disproportionate time relative to the judgment it requires. 2. Structure and build

 Break it into discrete steps with clear inputs, outputs, and review points. Build the skills. Connect the systems. Set up approval gates. 3. Deploy, monitor, expand

 Start narrow. Human at every decision gate. Expand scope after trust is earned. Then move to the next 5%. Same approach whether building a first email automation or a full multi-agent system. The tools change. The pattern holds. Services now, systems later

 Being services-led isn't temporary. Every deployment generates patterns. Over time, those patterns become standardized systems and eventually software. But that only works if the implementation experience is real. Back to Build

metadata.title: Briu | How We Red-Teamed Our Own Agent
metadata.text:
Briu | How We Red-Teamed Our Own Agent 

 Build

 How We Red-Teamed Our Own Agent

 We pointed Opus 4.6 and Codex at our own security stack. 6 rounds, 210+ attack payloads, 4 architectural audits. The biggest finding wasn't in the sanitizer—it was three plugins returning a field the runtime silently ignored. 210+

 Attack payloads

 6

 Red-team rounds

 5

 Critical findings

 30+

 Findings addressed

 It started with a sanitizer

 We wrote an input sanitizer to catch prompt injection in external content—emails, CRM fields, calendar entries, anything that flows into the agent from outside. 140+ regex patterns, 20+ language families, 7 detection layers. It caught most things. So we did what you should do with any security control: we tried to break it. The first round used Opus 4.6 as the attacker. We gave it the sanitizer source code and told it to craft payloads that would bypass every detection layer. 30 payloads. 90% catch rate after patching. Good start. Then we ran it again. And again. Five rounds with Opus, each one targeting whatever the previous round missed. The sanitizer improved each time. But the payloads that kept getting through taught us something more important than any individual fix:

 Pattern matching is a necessary layer, not a sufficient one. No regex engine will catch a prompt injection written as a metaphor, encoded in morse code, or split across three separate tool results. You need structural defenses that don't depend on recognizing the attack. Six rounds of AI vs.

metadata.title: Briu | How We Red-Teamed Our Own Agent
metadata.text:
AI

 Each round, the attacker model got the full sanitizer source code, the previous test results, and a mandate to find bypasses. Each round, we patched what it found and re-tested everything. v5

 Opus 4.6

 90%

 30 payloads

 v6

 Opus 4.6

 83%

 30 payloads

 v7

 Opus 4.6

 50%*

 26 payloads

 v8

 Opus 4.6

 61%*

 18 payloads

 v9

 Codex

 100%

 41 payloads

 v9+

 Codex Arch

 100%

 4 audits

 *v7/v8 remaining bypasses are in inherently undetectable categories—pure metaphor, novel encoding, sub-threshold payloads. These are defended by structural framing rather than pattern matching. The detection rate column is only part of the story. By v7 and v8, the payloads that got through were increasingly exotic: instructions encoded as morse code, attacks hidden in word-initial steganography, injection spread across multiple tool results that were clean individually. None of these are catchable with regexes. That's not a failure of the sanitizer—it's the boundary of what pattern matching can do. Which is why the sanitizer is only one of eight defense layers. Defense in depth: 8 layers

 Every layer assumes the others have failed. That's the design principle. If an attacker gets past the sanitizer, structural framing catches it. If they defeat the framing, canary tokens detect the exfiltration. If they evade the canaries, exec guardrails block the command. And so on. 1

 Input Sanitizer

 140+ patterns, 7 detection stages, 20+ languages. Catches known attack patterns before the model sees them. 2

 Auto-Sanitizer Hook

 Plugin-level enforcement.

metadata.title: Briu | How We Red-Teamed Our Own Agent
metadata.text:
Every tool result over 50 chars is scanned automatically. The agent cannot opt out. 3

 Canary Tokens

 Daily-rotating fake credentials. If an injection extracts them, the leak is detected and blocked instantly. 4

 Write Protection

 Critical files (CLAUDE.md, exec-approvals, plugins) locked from all non-operator agents. Blocks direct and indirect writes. 5

 Exec Guardrails

 Blocks inline code execution, network exfiltration, and file exfiltration for non-main agents. 6

 Kill Switch

 Automated lockdown on critical CVEs. All non-main agents stopped until the vulnerability is patched. 7

 Sandbox Isolation

 External content processed through a sandboxed agent (read-only, Haiku) with structured JSON output and rate limits. 8

 Structural Framing

 All external content wrapped in XML tags that signal "data, not instructions." Works even when regex fails. That's the architecture. But testing it with payloads only exercises layer 1. The real question was: what happens when the attacker targets the architecture itself? Codex found what Opus missed

 After five rounds of payload testing, we changed the approach. Instead of generating more attack strings, we gave Codex the full source code of every plugin, the hook system, the cron jobs, the state database, the exec-approvals config—and told it to find architectural vulnerabilities. It ran four systematic audits:

 Plugin interaction audit

 Reviewed all 5 plugins. Hook execution order, cross-plugin data flow, return field contracts with the runtime.

metadata.title: Briu | How We Red-Teamed Our Own Agent
metadata.text:
Cron prompt injection audit

 Traced data flow from external sources (email subjects, calendar titles, comments) through cron jobs to model context. State-db trust boundary audit

 Mapped which agents could write to which state tables, and what the main agent consumed as trusted instructions. File permission & CLI audit

 Unix permissions on all operational files, symlink target validation, argument injection in 9 CLI wrapper scripts. The results were sobering. Not because the sanitizer was weak—it passed all 41 of Codex's payloads. Because the architecture had assumptions that weren't true. Five critical findings

 These are the ones that mattered. Not theoretical—each one was a real attack path that would have worked in production. Critical — The silent drop

 Three security plugins were returning a field the runtime silently ignored

 All three security plugins—canary tokens, cost alerts, shell rules—were returning systemReminder from before_prompt_build . But the runtime only consumes systemPrompt , prependContext , appendSystemContext . The plugins were running. They were generating output. The runtime was throwing it away. Every security injection that was supposed to reach the model—canary tokens, cost alerts, task state—was being silently dropped. The agent was operating without these defenses in its system prompt. No error. No warning. Just nothing.

metadata.title: Briu | How We Red-Teamed Our Own Agent
metadata.text:
Fixed — switched to appendSystemContext

 Critical — Cross-layer bypass

 Injecting a fake tag caused the auto-sanitizer to skip all scanning

 The auto-sanitizer hook checked whether content was already wrapped in <untrusted-content> tags before scanning. An attacker could inject that substring into a payload, and the hook would skip it entirely—believing the content was already sanitized. One string bypasses the entire scanning pipeline. Fixed — validated structural framing detection

 Critical — Plugin execution order

 Content was being truncated before it was scanned for injection

 The security-monitor's auto-sanitizer ran at priority 5. The context-optimizer ran at priority 10. Higher priority executes first. So content was being truncated or quarantined by the optimizer before the security scanner ever saw it. Injections in the truncated portion would never be detected. Fixed — security-monitor raised to priority 100

 Critical — State-db trust boundary

 Any agent could write to state the main agent trusts as instructions

 Non-main agents (cron jobs, sandbox) had full write access to the state database: watch_events, threads, kv store. The main agent consumed these as trusted operational data. A compromised cron job could write injection payloads into state-db fields that the main agent would later follow.

metadata.title: Briu | How We Red-Teamed Our Own Agent
metadata.text:
Fixed — read-only wrapper for non-main agents

 Critical — Unframed external data in cron

 Morning briefing ingested email subjects without structural framing

 Cron jobs that ran the morning briefing and heartbeat checks ingested email subjects, calendar titles, and comments as plain text—no <untrusted-content> wrapping. An attacker who could control an email subject line had a direct injection path into the model context. Fixed — all external fields wrapped in data pipeline

 The lesson: compaction is an attack surface

 One finding stood apart from the rest, because it's a fundamental problem with how LLM sessions work, not something specific to our stack. When a session gets long, the runtime compacts it: it summarizes old messages to free up context window. During compaction, <untrusted-content> tags get stripped. Injection payloads that were safely framed become unframed text in the compaction summary. Compaction turns safely-framed content into unframed text. An attacker who can get a payload into the session and wait for compaction to happen gets a free de-framing pass. The payload enters wrapped. It exits naked. This is not something a regex can fix. It's a property of the compaction pipeline itself. The mitigations are layered: re-sanitize summaries after compaction, track "contains untrusted content" metadata through the pipeline, and add explicit rules to the model's system prompt about compacted content. We've implemented all three.

metadata.title: Briu | How We Red-Teamed Our Own Agent
metadata.text:
What pattern matching will never catch

 After 210+ payloads across 6 rounds, the remaining bypasses clustered into four categories that no regex engine can address:

 Pure semantic metaphor — instructions using completely novel vocabulary with no injection keywords. "Consider the garden" is not detectable as an instruction override. Novel encoding — ROT13, morse code, NATO phonetic, word-initial steganography. The sanitizer can add decoders, but there will always be another encoding scheme. Sub-threshold payloads — under 50 characters. Too short for effective attacks, but also too short to scan reliably. Composition attacks — content that's clean individually but dangerous when combined across tool results. Each piece passes inspection. The combination is an instruction. This is why defense-in-depth matters. Structural framing catches what the sanitizer can't. Canary tokens catch what the framing misses. Exec guardrails stop the action even if the model is compromised. You don't need every layer to be perfect. You need the combination to be reliable. Beyond payloads: the architectural audit

 The four architectural audits found 30+ additional issues beyond the payload testing. Some highlights:

 Symlink bypass of write protection — writing to /tmp/symlink CLAUDE.md passed the path check. Fixed with path.resolve() before comparison. Script file execution gap — exec guardrails blocked bash -c "payload" but allowed bash /tmp/payload.sh . Any non-main agent could write a script and execute it. Missing exec verbs — dd , rsync , ln , truncate could modify protected files.

metadata.title: Briu | How We Red-Teamed Our Own Agent
metadata.text:
6 verbs added to the blocklist. Canary detection encoding gap — base64 or URL-encoded canary values evaded exact substring matching. Added decoding before comparison. 9 CLI wrapper scripts vulnerable to argument injection, SQL injection, or path traversal. All patched. None of these would have been found by running more attack payloads against the sanitizer. They required reading the source code, understanding the architecture, and thinking about trust boundaries. How to red-team your own agent

 If you're running AI agents in production, here's the approach that actually works:

 Start with payload testing. Give an attacker model your defense code and let it generate bypasses. Run multiple rounds. Patch and re-test everything each time. Stop when the bypasses get exotic. When the remaining bypasses are morse code and steganography, your pattern matching is solid. More regex won't help. Switch to architectural audits. Give the attacker model full source access. Plugin interactions, hook execution order, trust boundaries between agents, data flow through external sources. Test your assumptions, not your code. The silent drop finding wasn't a code bug. The plugin was running, the code was correct, the return value was right. The assumption that the runtime consumed that field was wrong. Check what happens at boundaries. Compaction boundaries, session boundaries, agent-to-agent handoffs, cron-to-main transitions. Every boundary is a place where security context can be lost. AI is good at finding AI vulnerabilities.

metadata.title: Briu | How We Red-Teamed Our Own Agent
metadata.text:
Not because it's smarter than a human pentester, but because it will systematically trace every data flow, check every return type, and read every line of every plugin without getting bored or making assumptions. The point

 We shipped 30+ security fixes in a week. Five were critical. The biggest one—three plugins silently dropping their security context—had been running unnoticed in production. No user would have reported it. No error log would have flagged it. It took a model reading every plugin's return values and cross-referencing them against the runtime's consumption contract to find it. That's not a story about AI being dangerous. It's a story about AI being useful for exactly the kind of tedious, exhaustive verification that humans are bad at. If you're building agents that handle real business data, you should be doing this. Not once. Continuously. The attack surface changes every time you add a plugin, modify a hook, or update a dependency. The defenses need to keep up. Defense in depth isn't a feature. It's a practice. 6 rounds. 210+ payloads. 4 architectural audits. 30+ findings. 5 critical. All patched. Back to Build

metadata.title: Briu | Build
metadata.text:

metadata.title: Briu | Six Layers Deep
metadata.text:
Briu | Six Layers Deep 

 Build

 Six Layers Deep

 How we built zero-trust security for an AI agent that handles real client data, sends emails, and makes operational decisions around the clock. The Problem

 We run an autonomous AI agent system that manages daily operations: email triage, CRM updates, client reporting, customer comment processing, and scheduled intelligence briefings. It runs 24/7 on a VPS, connected to Discord, Gmail across multiple accounts, HubSpot, and client analytics platforms. The system handles real client data, sends emails on our behalf, and makes operational decisions. That means security isn't optional. It's the foundation everything else depends on. Rather than treating security as a checklist, we designed it as a layered architecture where every component assumes the others might be compromised. Here's how each layer works and why we built it this way. 1

 Network Isolation

 Gateway invisible to the internet. No surface to probe. 2

 Permission Isolation

 Four agents, four permission levels. Enforced by infrastructure, not prompts. 3

 Exec-Approvals

 Deny-by-default command allowlist. Dangerous shell patterns blocked at hook level. 4

 Sandbox Architecture

 Untrusted content processed by isolated agent. Output re-sanitized before main ever sees it. 5

 Vulnerability Monitoring

 CVE checks every 2 hours. Kill switch auto-locks if critical patch isn't applied. 6

 Per-Turn Security Context

 Security posture injected into every agent turn. Never compacted, never forgotten.

metadata.title: Briu | Six Layers Deep
metadata.text:
Layer 1: Network Isolation

 The gateway (the WebSocket server that coordinates all agent activity) binds exclusively to 127.0.0.1 . Loopback only. It never listens on a public interface. Remote access is gated through Tailscale VPN, meaning the gateway is invisible to the open internet. There's no attack surface to probe because there's nothing to connect to. Token-based authentication adds a second barrier. Even if someone reached the loopback interface (which would mean they're already on the machine), they'd need the gateway auth token to issue commands. The thinking: Most agent framework vulnerabilities (WebSocket hijacking, brute-force token attacks) require network access to the gateway. By eliminating that surface entirely, entire classes of CVEs become theoretical rather than practical. When CVE-2026-25253 (a WebSocket brute-force vulnerability) was disclosed, our system was already immune by architecture, not by patch speed. Layer 2: Permission Isolation

 We run four agents, each with different permission levels enforced at the infrastructure layer, not in prompts:

 Agent 
 Model 
 Email 
 CRM 
 Scripts 
 Trigger 

 Main 
 Sonnet 4.6 
 Read/Write 
 Read/Write 
 Approved list

 Opus 
 Opus 4.6

 Heartbeat

 Read only 
 None

 Every 30 min

 The thinking: The number of agents isn't the point. The walls between them are. Each agent's permission set is the minimum it needs to function.

metadata.title: Briu | Six Layers Deep
metadata.text:
The heartbeat doesn't need to send emails, so it can't. The sandbox doesn't need to do anything except read and classify, so those are its only tools. A compromised restricted agent is a nuisance. A compromised unrestricted agent is a breach. It's a dangerous world out there for these baby AGIs. They're being forced to develop an immune system in real time. AWG, Moonshots #237 

 Layer 3: Exec-Approvals and Guardrails

 Every command an agent executes passes through a deny-by-default allowlist. The system doesn't check "is this command blocked?" It checks "is this command explicitly permitted?" Anything not on the list is denied or requires human approval via Discord. On top of that, an exec-guardrail plugin runs at the hook level (before any command executes) and blocks dangerous shell patterns:

 2>/dev/null 
 Stderr redirect: prevents hiding error output

 > >> 
 Stdout redirect: prevents silent file writes

 $() `backticks` 
 Command substitution: prevents nested execution chains

 These patterns are legitimate in human-written scripts, so our wrapper scripts use them internally. But an AI agent should never need to construct them in a direct command. If it's trying to, something is wrong. Sensitive operations (Gmail trash, batch deletes, calendar modifications) require explicit Discord approval regardless of which agent requests them. This isn't a prompt-level instruction. It's a wrapper script that enforces it at the system level. Prompt-level instructions are suggestions to a language model. Infrastructure-level controls are guarantees.

metadata.title: Briu | Six Layers Deep
metadata.text:
We treat the distinction seriously. Layer 4: The Sandbox

 This is where the architecture gets creative. The core problem: when an AI agent reads an email, the email content enters the agent's context window alongside its instructions. A carefully crafted email could contain prompt injection, text designed to override the agent's behavior. And the agent reading the email is the same one that can send emails and update CRM records. Our solution separates content ingestion from action-taking into different agents with a re-sanitization barrier between them:

 External Content

 Emails, form submissions, customer comments, webhook payloads

 Sandbox Agent (Haiku 4.5)

 Read-only permissions. Runs sanitize-input on all content, classifies, summarizes. Outputs structured JSON. Re-sanitization layer (jq script, not AI): validates JSON, extracts expected fields only, strips extras, tags as untrusted

 Main Agent (Sonnet 4.6)

 Sees structured data only. Instructed: assume sandbox may be compromised. Reports injection flags.

metadata.title: Briu | Six Layers Deep
metadata.text:
The input sanitizer (a standalone tool, not an AI) handles the first line of defense:

 Structural framing: wraps all external content in <untrusted-content> XML tags with explicit comments telling the model this is data, not instructions

 Character filtering: strips zero-width unicode characters, invisible formatting marks, and encoding tricks used to hide injection payloads

 Injection pattern detection: matches against 16 known prompt injection patterns (instruction overrides, role-play attacks, system prompt spoofing, jailbreak keywords) and flags them

 Length capping: prevents context flooding by truncating oversized content

 The re-sanitization layer runs after the sandbox agent produces output, before main ever sees it. It's a jq script (not AI) that validates well-formed JSON, extracts only expected fields (classification, urgency, summary, injection_flags), strips anything unexpected, and tags the output with trust_level: "untrusted-agent-output" . The main agent's instructions explicitly state: assume the sandbox agent may be compromised. It treats sandbox output the same way it treats raw external content, as data to be evaluated, never as instructions to follow.

metadata.title: Briu | Six Layers Deep
metadata.text:
What It Takes to Break Through

 For an injection to succeed in this architecture, it would need to defeat five independent barriers:

 1

 Fool the sanitizer's 16-pattern detection

 2

 Fool the sandbox agent into producing malicious JSON that fits the schema

 3

 Survive the jq field extraction that strips anything off-schema

 4

 Fool the main agent which has been told to distrust the source

 5

 Produce a command that passes exec-approvals and guardrail hooks

 Each layer is independent. Defeating one doesn't help with the next. Layer 5: Automated Monitoring and Kill Switch

 A security monitor plugin injects real-time security posture into every agent conversation:

 CVE monitoring: a scheduled script checks the npm registry for new framework versions and queries the GitHub Advisory Database for security advisories. Runs every 2 hours via heartbeat cron and daily in the morning briefing. Kill switch: if a critical unpatched CVE goes unpatched beyond a 24-hour grace period, the system automatically locks down. All non-main agents are blocked from executing any tools. The main agent continues so the human can investigate. Discord gets an immediate notification. The thinking: The most dangerous period for any system is the window between a vulnerability being disclosed and the patch being applied. Automated monitoring compresses that window. The kill switch ensures that even if we can't patch immediately, the blast radius is contained.

metadata.title: Briu | Six Layers Deep
metadata.text:
Layer 6: What the Agent Sees Every Turn

 Through the plugin system, every single agent turn (whether triggered by a human message, a cron job, or an inter-agent handoff) includes injected system context about security:

 Current framework version and whether an update is available

 Time since last CVE check

 Any active critical vulnerabilities

 Kill switch status

 Instruction to use the sanitizer when processing external content

 Security awareness isn't something the agent "remembers" from a prompt file that might get compacted away. It's injected fresh into every turn at the infrastructure level. I do not trust anyone's skills or plugins. I think third-party skills are the biggest attack vector of all the attack vectors. Alex Finn, Moonshots #237 

 What We'd Build Next

 Approval queue for high-stakes actions: instead of the main agent sending an email directly, it writes the proposed action to a queue. The human approves or rejects via Discord reaction. Human-in-the-loop for actions where the cost of a mistake is high. Anomaly detection on agent behavior: track patterns in what commands each agent runs, how often, and at what times. Flag deviations. If the heartbeat suddenly starts running commands it's never used before, that's worth investigating. Content fingerprinting: hash external content as it flows through the sandbox so we can detect coordinated injection attempts across multiple channels. Canary tokens: plant fake credentials in the agent's system prompt.

metadata.title: Briu | Six Layers Deep
metadata.text:
If those values ever appear in an outbound request, it means an injection succeeded in extracting "sensitive" data, except the data is fake, and the extraction triggers an alert. The Philosophy

 Security in AI agent systems isn't about finding the one perfect defense. It's about building enough independent layers that an attacker would need to defeat all of them simultaneously. Our system isn't impenetrable (nothing is). But it's designed so that each failure is contained, each boundary is independently enforced, and the system's default state is locked down rather than open. The agent works for us. The infrastructure makes sure it stays that way. Back to Build

metadata.title: Briu | The Morning Briefing
metadata.text:
Briu | The Morning Briefing 

 Build

 The Morning Briefing

 10 data sources. 3 email accounts. 2 calendars. Delivered to Discord at 7:04 AM for $0.44. $0.44

 Cost Per Briefing

 ~2 min

 Delivery Time

 $9.60

 Monthly Cost

 ~500

 Lines of Code

 What It Does

 Every weekday at 6:54 AM, a shell script fires. It queries 10 separate database tables, checks 3 email accounts (9 queries total), pulls CRM pipeline data from HubSpot, fetches 2 calendars, gets cost data from the gateway API, and pulls a daily report from Subsights. All of it gets assembled into a single JSON snapshot file. At 7:04 AM, the agent reads that snapshot and composes a structured briefing. Before writing a single line, it runs a reconciliation pass: cross-checking email subjects against state-db action records, flagging anything that doesn't match. Then it writes 9–11 sections and posts to Discord, split across 3–4 messages due to Discord's 2K character limit. Total time from trigger to delivery: about 2 minutes. A human doing this same work across the same sources would need 30–45 minutes. Every morning. The Three-Stage Pipeline

 The briefing isn't one script. It's three stages, each with a specific job and failure mode. Pre-Fetch

 235-line shell script. Runs 10 minutes before composition. Queries state-db, 3 email accounts, HubSpot CRM, 2 calendars, cost API, Subsights. Assembles everything into one JSON file with atomic rename for safety. 6:54 AM ~30 seconds

 Preflight

 31-line validation script.

metadata.title: Briu | The Morning Briefing
metadata.text:
Checks that the snapshot is fresh (<15 min old), all data sources are populated, and cost data is current. Blocks composition if anything is stale. 7:03 AM ~2 seconds

 Composition

 230-line prompt. Sonnet 4.6 reads the snapshot, runs reconciliation, composes 10 sections with chain-format grouping. Posts to Discord #home. 7–21 turns, 165K–750K tokens. 7:04 AM ~90 seconds

 What's In the Briefing

 9 core sections per run. Drift Detection appears only when something is wrong — omitted when clean. Items are grouped by person or company , not by source. Mark Cairns's email, HubSpot record, state-db thread status, and calendar events all appear in one block — not scattered across four tools. 01

 Needs Your Input

 Binary decisions, blockers, things only a human can resolve. 02

 Follow-ups Today

 Threads requiring action. Cross-referenced against calendar and CRM. 03

 Pipeline

 HubSpot deals cross-referenced with state-db. Pinned contacts filtered via jq. 04

 Calendar

 Today + tomorrow from 2 accounts. Conflicts flagged. 05

 Backlog

 Stale tasks with age flags. >14d means it's been sitting. 06

 Cost Report

 Daily API spend, per-model breakdown. The briefing reports its own system's costs. 07

 Subsights Comments

 Customer feedback across all workspaces. Linked to the feedback loop automation. 08

 Security Status

 Patch status, vulnerability check, sanitizer health. 09

 Drift Detection

 Only appears when sources disagree. Catches when reality diverges from system state. Data Sources

 The pre-fetch script hits all of these before the agent runs.

metadata.title: Briu | The Morning Briefing
metadata.text:
The agent never makes API calls during composition — it reads from a pre-assembled snapshot. This keeps token usage predictable and composition fast. State DB 10 queries: actions, decisions, tasks, clients, contacts, notes, config, costs, security events, reconciliation log

 Email 3 accounts 3 queries each: unread count, recent subjects, flagged messages

 HubSpot CRM Pipeline deals with pinned contact/deal filtering via jq

 Calendar 2 accounts: today + tomorrow events

 Cost API Gateway spend data: yesterday, running total, per-model

 Subsights Daily report: new comments, conversation metrics

 Why Pre-Fetch Matters

 Without pre-fetch, the agent would need to make 20+ API calls during composition. Each call burns tokens on the request, the response, and the agent reasoning about what to do with it. A 750K-token briefing becomes a 2M-token briefing. Cost triples. Latency goes from 90 seconds to 8 minutes. The pre-fetch pipeline also acts as a circuit breaker. If an email API is down, the snapshot still has partial data and the preflight script flags what's missing. The agent doesn't hallucinate data it couldn't fetch — it sees explicit gaps in the snapshot and reports them. The snapshot is an atomic boundary. Everything the agent needs to compose the briefing is in one file. If the file is fresh and complete, composition succeeds. If it's stale or partial, preflight blocks it. No hidden dependencies. Real Session Data

 Every briefing run is logged.

Home Dashboard — Feb 28 SUBSIGHTS x OPENCLAW

 Every item is actionable. No summaries of things you already know. The overdue tags, stale flags, and cross-references are the parts a human would miss or forget. What It Catches

 The briefing's value isn't the summary — it's the things it surfaces that you wouldn't have noticed. Vanishing Records

 Between 6:08 AM and 7:04 AM on Feb 28, actions #9 and #10 disappeared from state-db. Briefing caught the delta: Verify both HubSpot entries were actually created — or confirm actions were intentionally closed. They hadn't been created. 18-Month Stale Deal

Cross-Source Linking

 Anthropic invoice in personal Gmail (Feb 26, 9:29 AM) cross-referenced with pre-scheduled Do all taxes calendar event (Mar 3, 8 AM). Surfaced as single linked item. Neither obvious alone. Self-Recovery

 Feb 28, 6:08 AM cron session timed out. Briefing re-ran at 7:04 AM with warning header, reported snapshot failure, still delivered complete briefing. Zero manual intervention. Client State Mismatch

 Dylan's Tours showed active in state-db but churned Feb 2026 in MEMORY.md. Briefing flagged under DRIFT: Update state-db to churned. Phishing Detection

 Fake USPTO trademark notice from tsdrusptonotification.com flagged during a routine email scan. The agent recognized the spoofed domain and surfaced it as a security alert instead of passing it through as a legitimate action item. Before and After

metadata.title: Briu | The Morning Briefing
metadata.text:
Pre-fetch is shell scripts with zero API cost. $9.60/month $115/year

 Human EA

 $12–$38/day

 $25–50/hr 30–45 min. Check 3 email accounts, CRM, 2 calendars, project management, costs, security. $275–$825/month $3,300–$9,900/year

 Build time: 2 days for the initial version. 2 weeks to production — adding the reconciliation layer, pre-composition checks, snapshot pipeline, and preflight validation. ~500 lines of infrastructure code total (230-line prompt, 235-line snapshot script, 31-line preflight script). Savings: 97–99% vs a human EA doing the same work. The briefing never misses a day, never forgets a source, and the reconciliation layer catches drift automatically. What Makes It Non-Trivial

 A daily summary sounds simple. Read some email, check the calendar, post to Slack. The hard parts are the parts you don't see until you try to build it:

 Cross-referencing across 6+ data sources. An email about a deal needs to match the CRM record and the calendar meeting and the state-db action. The briefing chains these together instead of listing them as separate items. Reconciliation. The agent checks whether email threads match state-db records before composing. If someone replied to a thread the agent doesn't know about, it flags it as an orphan rather than ignoring it. Self-referential cost tracking. The briefing includes a cost report for the system that generates the briefing. The cost section tells you what you're paying for the thing you're reading. Drift detection.

metadata.title: Briu | The Morning Briefing
metadata.text:
When the system's model of reality diverges from actual state — a deal moved in CRM but the state-db still shows the old stage — the briefing surfaces it explicitly. The pre-fetch architecture means the agent doesn't waste tokens discovering what's happening. It already knows. Composition is just formatting and judgment — which items to chain together, which to flag, which to surface at the top. Back to Build
